item.title: Re: Some factual issues here:
item.content:encoded:
1. Fixed, Nanjing wasn't even supposed to be there in the first place, that was an error. My brain blipped when writing that, and had that name in mind
2. It's purposeful, it's a rough translation I'll state that in article.
3. Fair enough, I swapped it.

item.title: Some factual issues here:
item.content:encoded:
1. Nanjing is not a part of Shanghai and Nanjing is not a coastal city. There is only a road called Nanjing in Shanghai.
2. The messages scratched into the shipwreck seems to be in English, given the mistakes here. Was that originally in English or was it a translation?
3. The total sea area of Shanghai is only 10000 square kilometers and it is not a traditionally a region for shark finning; maybe consider using a coastal city of Guangdong province instead?

item.title: Re: Hey!
item.content:encoded: Oooooooohhohhh, you were also in the irc! I love me some SPC, but you made it sad? Teach me your powers.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Full Licensing Information:

The image that is used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public Domain
Title: NPM Shark Fin Soup.JPG
Author: (WT-en) Ash rex
Release year: 2007

item.title: Why and how.
item.content:encoded:
Okay so I struggled with the how, moreso but I'll answer in order
For the why, I've stated before it's for the sport of hunting, hence why the message in the article says: "Happy hunting!" Also what is the shark punching center without sharks.

For the how, its the shark trying to get the person to stop by trying to show it's human. For the first punch, the shark swims away into the ship, hence the "I got punch ed" for the second one, the puncher isn't as fast a shark, so you can envision it like this: the shark swimming away to scratch the message trying to prove it was human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The logic behind this is confusing. If I understand correctly, the soup was created by a front of the Shark Punching Center so the Shark Punching Center could Punch Sharks. But that seems… counter productive. Why would they turn people into sharks just to punch them when there are actual sharks that need punching right now?

I also took issue with the notes scratched into the boats. They don't really add anything to the article and err towards repetition. Also, how is a shark going to find time to scratch something into a boat when it is being punched?

item.title: SCP-5450
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. It's a simple enough article with a good progression. I'd like to see there be more of a mystery around what SCP-5450-1 is personally, but that's just me projecting what I'd do with the concept. Like has been suggested, there are some writing changes that could be made that would make it flow a lot better, but this is overall a good skip with an interesting premise and a good flow. Good job :)

item.title: Re: Hey!
item.content:encoded: Actually, the IUCN Red List has a scale based on the total population numbers of each animal which determines its conservation status.

item.title: Re: Hey!
item.content:encoded:
Can I suggest a few grammatical/structural changes that might make the early part of the article flow better?

+ Open
- Close

Description: SCP-5450 is a recipe known as shark fin soup, which manifests anomalous properties when produced from sharks within the Shanghai region of China. Selachians within this area, now designated SCP-5450-1, are biologically indistinguishable from normal sharks barring the presence of a pair of lungs. The anomalous property of SCP-5450 manifests upon consumption, causing the subject to transform into a species of selachian. Following transformation, the subject will find its way to a body of water via currently unknown means. A small number of cases have been observed to move their fins in a manner similar to walking. Testing has been preemptively denied by Site Director Jorge Clark as the effects of SCP-5450 are well documented and apparent.

Discovery: SCP-5450 was discovered when several families went missing in Shanghai followed by several reports of walking sharks online. Witnesses to instances of SCP-5450-1 were promptly amnesticized. As of 06/07/2019, there are estimated to be 452 instances of SCP-5450-1, making them an endangered species.


Also, they wouldn't really be an endangered species, because that's a designation usually given by a governmental organization, which the Foundation isn't.

item.title: Hey!
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for reading my article. This is in my efforts to make all my Jamcon Entries GoI related.

+ Acknowledgements
- Close

Thanks to these people you made this happen!
Zoobeeny Generally the one who sent me in a better direction, and made it wayyyyy sadder.
Doctor Fullham Encouragement, SPaG, anomaly Thanks!
DrAkimoto SPaG, encouragement.

+ Image Source
– hide block

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f0/NPM_Shark_Fin_Soup.JPG

Thanks for reading, hope you enjoyed!
